root_text.an3.156:1.3: Āgāḷhā paṭipadā, nijjhāmā paṭipadā, majjhimā paṭipadā.
root_text.an3.156:1.5: Idha, bhikkhave, ekacco evaṁvādī hoti evaṁdiṭṭhi:
root_text.an3.156:2.2: Idha, bhikkhave, ekacco acelako hoti muttācāro, hatthāpalekhano, naehibhadantiko, natiṭṭhabhadantiko, nābhihaṭaṁ na uddissakataṁ na nimantanaṁ sādiyati.
root_text.an3.156:2.3: So na kumbhimukhā paṭiggaṇhāti, na kaḷopimukhā paṭiggaṇhāti na eḷakamantaraṁ na daṇḍamantaraṁ na musalamantaraṁ na dvinnaṁ bhuñjamānānaṁ na gabbhiniyā na pāyamānāya na purisantaragatāya na saṅkittīsu na yattha sā upaṭṭhito hoti na yattha makkhikā saṇḍasaṇḍacārinī na macchaṁ na maṁsaṁ na suraṁ na merayaṁ, na thusodakaṁ pivati.
root_text.an3.156:2.4: So ekāgāriko vā hoti ekālopiko, dvāgāriko vā hoti dvālopiko … sattāgāriko vā hoti sattālopiko;
root_text.an3.156:2.5: ekissāpi dattiyā yāpeti, dvīhipi dattīhi yāpeti … sattahipi dattīhi yāpeti;
root_text.an3.156:2.6: ekāhikampi āhāraṁ āhāreti, dvāhikampi āhāraṁ āhāreti … sattāhikampi āhāraṁ āhāreti—iti evarūpaṁ addhamāsikampi pariyāyabhattabhojanānuyogamanuyutto viharati.
root_text.an3.156:3.1: So sākabhakkhopi hoti, sāmākabhakkhopi hoti, nīvārabhakkhopi hoti, daddulabhakkhopi hoti, haṭabhakkhopi hoti, kaṇabhakkhopi hoti, ācāmabhakkhopi hoti, piññākabhakkhopi hoti, tiṇabhakkhopi hoti, gomayabhakkhopi hoti, vanamūlaphalāhāro yāpeti pavattaphalabhojī.
root_text.an3.156:4.1: So sāṇānipi dhāreti, masāṇānipi dhāreti, chavadussānipi dhāreti, paṁsukūlānipi dhāreti, tirīṭānipi dhāreti, ajinampi dhāreti, ajinakkhipampi dhāreti, kusacīrampi dhāreti, vākacīrampi dhāreti, phalakacīrampi dhāreti, kesakambalampi dhāreti, vāḷakambalampi dhāreti, ulūkapakkhikampi dhāreti,
root_text.an3.156:4.7: iti evarūpaṁ anekavihitaṁ kāyassa ātāpanaparitāpanānuyogamanuyutto viharati.
root_text.an3.156:5.2: Idha, bhikkhave, bhikkhu kāye kāyānupassī viharati ātāpī sampajāno satimā vineyya loke abhijjhādomanassaṁ;
root_text.an3.156:5.5: dhammesu dhammānupassī viharati ātāpī sampajāno satimā vineyya loke abhijjhādomanassaṁ.
root_text.an3.157:1.3: Āgāḷhā paṭipadā, nijjhāmā paṭipadā, majjhimā paṭipadā.
root_text.an3.157:3.2: Idha, bhikkhave, bhikkhu anuppannānaṁ pāpakānaṁ akusalānaṁ dhammānaṁ anuppādāya chandaṁ janeti vāyamati vīriyaṁ ārabhati cittaṁ paggaṇhāti padahati;
root_text.an3.157:3.3: uppannānaṁ pāpakānaṁ akusalānaṁ dhammānaṁ pahānāya chandaṁ janeti vāyamati vīriyaṁ ārabhati cittaṁ paggaṇhāti padahati;
root_text.an3.157:3.4: anuppannānaṁ kusalānaṁ dhammānaṁ uppādāya chandaṁ janeti vāyamati vīriyaṁ ārabhati cittaṁ paggaṇhāti padahati;
root_text.an3.157:3.5: uppannānaṁ kusalānaṁ dhammānaṁ ṭhitiyā asammosāya bhiyyobhāvāya vepullāya bhāvanāya pāripūriyā chandaṁ janeti vāyamati vīriyaṁ ārabhati cittaṁ paggaṇhāti padahati ….

translation_text.an3.156:1.3: The addicted practice, the scorching practice, and the middle practice.
translation_text.an3.156:1.5: It’s when someone has this doctrine and view:
translation_text.an3.156:1.7: so they throw themselves into sensual pleasures.
translation_text.an3.156:1.8: This is called the addicted practice.
translation_text.an3.156:2.2: It’s when someone goes naked, ignoring conventions. They lick their hands, and don’t come or wait when called. They don’t consent to food brought to them, or food prepared on their behalf, or an invitation for a meal.
translation_text.an3.156:2.3: They don’t receive anything from a pot or bowl; or from someone who keeps sheep, or who has a weapon or a shovel in their home; or where a couple is eating; or where there is a woman who is pregnant, breastfeeding, or who lives with a man; or where there’s a hound waiting or flies buzzing. They accept no fish or meat or beer or wine, and drink no fermented gruel.
translation_text.an3.156:2.4: They go to just one house for alms, taking just one mouthful, or two houses and two mouthfuls, up to seven houses and seven mouthfuls.
translation_text.an3.156:2.5: They feed on one saucer a day, two saucers a day, up to seven saucers a day.
translation_text.an3.156:2.6: They eat once a day, once every second day, up to once a week, and so on, even up to once a fortnight. They live pursuing the practice of eating food at set intervals.
translation_text.an3.156:3.1: They eat herbs, millet, wild rice, poor rice, water lettuce, rice bran, scum from boiling rice, sesame flour, grass, or cow dung. They survive on forest roots and fruits, or eating fallen fruit.
translation_text.an3.156:4.1: They wear robes of sunn hemp, mixed hemp, corpse-wrapping cloth, rags, lodh tree bark, antelope hide (whole or in strips), kusa grass, bark, wood-chips, human hair, horse-tail hair, or owls’ wings.
translation_text.an3.156:4.2: They tear out their hair and beard, committed to this practice.
translation_text.an3.156:4.3: They constantly stand, turning down seats.
translation_text.an3.156:4.4: They squat, committed to the endeavor of squatting.
translation_text.an3.156:4.5: They lie on a mat of thorns, making a mat of thorns their bed.
translation_text.an3.156:4.6: They’re devoted to ritual bathing three times a day, including at dusk.
translation_text.an3.156:4.7: And so they live pursuing these various ways of mortifying and tormenting the body.
translation_text.an3.156:4.8: This is called the scorching practice.
translation_text.an3.156:5.2: It’s when a mendicant meditates by observing an aspect of the body—keen, aware, and mindful, rid of covetousness and displeasure for the world.
translation_text.an3.156:5.3: They meditate observing an aspect of feelings …
translation_text.an3.156:5.4: They meditate observing an aspect of the mind …
translation_text.an3.156:5.5: They meditate observing an aspect of principles—keen, aware, and mindful, rid of covetousness and displeasure for the world.
translation_text.an3.156:5.6: This is called the middle practice.
translation_text.an3.157:1.3: The addicted practice, the scorching practice, the middle practice.
translation_text.an3.157:1.5: This is called the addicted practice.
translation_text.an3.157:2.1: And what is the scorching practice? …
translation_text.an3.157:2.2: This is called the scorching practice.
translation_text.an3.157:3.2: It’s when a mendicant generates enthusiasm, tries, makes an effort, exerts the mind, and strives so that bad, unskillful qualities don’t arise.
translation_text.an3.157:3.3: They generate enthusiasm, try, make an effort, exert the mind, and strive so that bad, unskillful qualities that have arisen are given up.
translation_text.an3.157:3.4: They generate enthusiasm, try, make an effort, exert the mind, and strive so that skillful qualities arise.
translation_text.an3.157:3.5: They generate enthusiasm, try, make an effort, exert the mind, and strive so that skillful qualities that have arisen remain, are not lost, but increase, mature, and are completed by development. …
translation_text.an3.158:1.1: They develop the basis of psychic power that has immersion due to enthusiasm, and active effort.
translation_text.an3.158:1.2: They develop the basis of psychic power that has immersion due to energy, and active effort.
translation_text.an3.158:1.3: They develop the basis of psychic power that has immersion due to mental development, and active effort.
translation_text.an3.158:1.4: They develop the basis of psychic power that has immersion due to inquiry, and active effort. …
translation_text.an3.159:1.1: They develop the faculty of faith …
translation_text.an3.160:1.1: They develop the power of faith …
translation_text.an3.161:1.1: They develop the awakening factor of mindfulness …
translation_text.an3.162:1.9: This is called the middle practice.
